sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A radiochemistry exercise tracked the activity of an isotope used in a diagnostic tracer simulation. Initial activity was 800 counts/s. After 6 hours it was 400 counts/s, after 12 hours it was 200 counts/s, and after 18 hours it was 100 counts/s. Students assumed first-order radioactive decay and ignored background radiation.

explanations.B: Correct. The activity falls from 800 to 400 counts/s in 6 hours and continues halving every 6 hours. The half-life is therefore 6 hours.
explanations.A: Three hours is not the time required for any listed halving.
explanations.C: Twelve hours is the time for two half-lives.
explanations.D: Eighteen hours is the time for three half-lives.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Activity of a radioactive isotope decreases by half every 6 hours.

questions.stem: What activity would be expected after 24 hours?

explanations.C: Correct. After 18 hours the activity is 100 counts/s. One more 6-hour half-life brings it to 50 counts/s at 24 hours.
explanations.A: 25 counts/s would occur after one additional half-life beyond 24 hours, at 30 hours.
explanations.B: 100 counts/s is the activity at 18 hours.
explanations.D: 200 counts/s is the activity at 12 hours.

questions.stem: Radioactive decay follows what kind of kinetic behavior?

explanations.A: Correct. Radioactive decay is first order: the rate of decay is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present. That is why half-life is constant.
explanations.B: Zero-order decay would lose a constant amount per time, not a constant fraction.
explanations.C: Second-order kinetics depend on collisions between reactant particles, not spontaneous nuclear decay.
explanations.D: Michaelis-Menten behavior describes enzyme-catalyzed reactions, not nuclear decay.

questions.stem: Why does the count rate decrease over time?

explanations.D: Correct. As nuclei decay, fewer unstable nuclei remain to emit radiation. Activity therefore decreases with time.
explanations.A: Detector mass is not the explanation for the decay curve.
explanations.B: The isotope is not becoming more concentrated as it decays.
explanations.C: For first-order radioactive decay, half-life is constant, not increasing every 6 hours.

questions.stem: What fraction of the initial activity remains after 18 hours?

explanations.C: Correct. Eighteen hours equals three 6-hour half-lives. After three half-lives, the remaining fraction is (1/2)³ = 1/8.
explanations.A: One half remains after one half-life, or 6 hours.
explanations.B: One fourth remains after two half-lives, or 12 hours.
explanations.D: One sixteenth remains after four half-lives, or 24 hours.

questions.passage: A physiology lab prepared glucose solutions separated from pure water by a semipermeable membrane permeable to water but not glucose. At 25°C, measured osmotic pressures for glucose concentrations of 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, and 0.40 M were approximately 2.5, 5.0, 7.4, and 9.9 atm. Glucose was treated as a nonelectrolyte, so i = 1 in π = iMRT.
questions.stem: What osmotic pressure is measured for 0.20 M glucose?

explanations.B: Correct. Figure 2 shows an osmotic pressure of about 5.0 atm at 0.20 M glucose. The relationship is approximately linear.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Osmotic pressure increases approximately linearly with glucose concentration.

choices.D: Glucose does not dissociate into ions in solution
choices.B: Glucose is always insoluble in water
choices.C: Glucose changes R in the gas constant

explanations.D: Correct. The van’t Hoff factor i counts dissolved particles per formula unit. Glucose remains as intact molecules in water, so i = 1.
explanations.A: Two ions would imply i ≈ 2, which is not true for glucose.
explanations.B: Glucose is soluble enough to prepare the solutions described.
explanations.C: The gas constant R is not changed by glucose.

questions.stem: If NaCl fully dissociated at the same molarity, how would its osmotic pressure compare with glucose?

choices.A: It would be about twice as large
choices.B: It would be half as large
choices.C: It would be the same because molarity is the same

explanations.A: Correct. NaCl ideally produces two particles per formula unit, so i ≈ 2. At the same molarity and temperature, π would be about twice that of glucose.
explanations.B: More particles increase osmotic pressure rather than halving it.
explanations.C: Osmotic pressure depends on particle concentration, not just formula-unit molarity.
explanations.D: A dissolved electrolyte would not have zero osmotic pressure.

questions.stem: Water will initially tend to move in which direction across the membrane?

choices.B: From pure water toward the glucose solution
choices.A: From the glucose solution toward pure water only
choices.C: Equally in both directions with no net movement
choices.D: Only if glucose crosses the membrane first

explanations.B: Correct. Water moves by osmosis toward the side with higher solute concentration when the solute cannot cross. The membrane is permeable to water but not glucose.
explanations.A: The net movement is toward the higher solute side, not away from it.
explanations.C: There may be molecular movement both ways, but the net flow is toward glucose solution.
explanations.D: Osmosis does not require glucose to cross the membrane.

questions.stem: Using R = 0.082 L·atm/mol·K and T = 298 K, what is π for 0.10 M glucose?

explanations.C: Correct. π = iMRT = 1 × 0.10 × 0.082 × 298 ≈ 2.44 atm, close to the measured 2.5 atm.
explanations.A: 0.24 atm is too small by a factor of 10.
explanations.B: 24 atm is too large by a factor of 10.
explanations.D: 298 atm incorrectly treats temperature as pressure without multiplying the full expression.
